messages.content:
Alternate version 2

A Doctor Who fanfiction, Basically a retold version of "Human Nature/The Family Of Blood", Also a crossover with KikoRiki

Name of this story: Doctor Who: Human Nature (feat. KikoRiki) 

Main characters: The 10th Doctor, Martha Jones

Once upon a time, there was a ram named Wally, who was adventuring through a rumoured haunted house near Cambridgeshire 

Wally figured that there was no such thing as ghosts, and so was content to push through the haunted house.  A man shot him in the side. He screamed in agony and ran away, leaving a trail of blood and screams behind him. He eventually fainted. The man dissapeared, he was part of a crime family known as the Family of Blood

The Family of Blood were after the Doctor's essence to stay alive

Meanwhile,

"Martha, you trust me don't you" "This watch is me" "I'm gonna become human" were words uttered by the elusive Time Lord, the Doctor, who was about to use the Chameleon Arch to rewrite his biology, changing a Time Lord into a human.

His faithful companion , Martha Jones, could not believe her eyes as the Doctor screamed in pain as he became human, He became a human named John Smith. The TARDIS landed in a cottage near St. Albans, Hertfordshire ,where Smith was going on a excursion to a haunted house.

John Smith spoke to Joan, a maid that "I've been having these weird dreams, I have 2 hearts and I am a Time Lord. They say that I'm a man known as the Doctor" Joan uses a stethoscope and confirms "You only have one heart and one soul".

John Smith kept his dreams in a journal called "The Journal of Impossible Things" Here he writes his past incarnations, the Daleks, the Cybermen and the Tardis.

The excursion began, When they got there John Smith was talking to Joan about his dreams, Meanwhile, Martha spotted a trail of blood, when she followed it, what she saw was disheartening,  apuddle of blood spreading in stripes across the stone floor in different directions. Martha walked forward to understand what was happening. In the very center of this puddle lay Wally. True, Martha did not know what kind of purple fluffy round thing with twisted horns and small hooves was, but realizing that it was from this body that blood was flowing, She sent John and Joan to see it. And of course they soon came running. But the picture they saw scared them.

"What happened, Martha?" - John Smith stopped and exclaimed at the same time, looking intently at what was lying on the floor.

“There’s some kind of furball lying around in the blood, we should help him,” Martha muttered.

Barash's body was picked up by levitation and carried somewhere, and Harry went to his room - to sleep.

All the KikoRiki sat down near Wally's bed.
Dokko the Moose, Chiko the Hedgehog and Rosa the Pig were sobbing. Barry the Bwar snorted loudly, blowing his nose into his handkerchief, as a nurse approached them.

“I don’t know how to treat him,” the woman muttered sadly.

- He will die? - Rosa screamed, throwing her eyes up.

— Yes.

Rosa began to cry harder, almost sobbing, but she tried to calm down.

- Inhale-exhale-inhale-exhale-inhale - Rosa died on from her last exhale, she died from shock.

Suddenly John Smith entered the room.

- Since they died, they must be buried. Joan!

- Yes,

- Order a coffin, we will have a funeral.

"It will be done, Mr. Smith."

The rest of the KikoRiki dispersed, waiting for the funeral to begin...

The funeral was quiet and took place in secret. Rosa and Wally were buried in a common coffin.

Olga the Owl wiped her tears with a handkerchief. Barry hugged her. Everyone was sad.

They had just lost 2 of their best friends.

Oh, you could see that Rosa was crying just before she died... she really loved Wally very much...

Later that night, the KikoRiki chose to stay at the cemetary, waiting until it was time to leave,

Meanwhile, Martha went to the TARDIS as John had fallen in love with Joan, She played a video where the Doctor says "Here's a list of instructions for when I'm human, Number 1 if they find us, you know what to do, Open the watch, I've put a perception filter on it so the human me won't think anything of it, to him it's just a fob watch, so he won't understand its significance, Open the watch and all the time lord essence will come out, But dont open it unless you have to, because then the Family of Blood will be able to find me. Number 2, don't let me abandon you, Number 3" Martha skipped through the video trying to find out what to do if the Doctor falls in love with a human, there was nothing" 

Back at the cottage, everyone was singing a memorial hymn for Wally and Rosa.

- We're gonna miss you, friends... 

They were talking about their favourite memories with them, John Smith was talking to Joan "It's amazing how these colorful animals act like humans, It's almost as if they are magical", Joan then said "John Smith, I love you", They hugged, much to Martha's dismay, because now John would never want to go back to being the Doctor..., The music began to play "Fairytale of New York", as they began thinking about Christmas and how they would have loved to spend it with Wally and Rosa.

A blue rabbit, Krash found the fob watch, He briefly opened it and saw flashes of the life of The Doctor, He saw the 9th doctor running to save Rose from the Annedroid,  the 10th doctor losing Rose to the alternate universe, the 1st doctor meeting the Daleks, even 7th regenerating into 8th, He quickly closed it with his left paw, This alerted the Family, and they appeared, Son of Mine, Mother of Mine, Father of Mine and Daughter of Mine, they had came to hunt him down...

Meanwhile, everyone else was crying in their hotel rooms, Krash went to see Olga who was crying at a photo of Rosa, She showed him the photo, and he showed Olga the watch, Meanwhile, the Family had begun a attack and they grabbed Martha and Joan. They began saying "Change Back, You've turned human" John Smith said "I was born human like the rest of you", Then a gun was pointed at Joan and Martha "Which is going to die, Your friend?or your lover, Your choice". Just then, they were grabbed and saved by a blue paw, it was Krash, who ran them to safety and said "Those things, they want you, They say you're a hero, I gotta go see Chiko" He runs away, Meanwhile, they see the Tardis, John Smith says "I've never seen that before in my life"

Later, a person was going to the library with the invisibility cloak, He saw Chiko crying, He was still mourning the deaths of Wally and Rosa, and Chiko didn't understand why they had to go to the funeral...

Barry was going to the Herbology block, to study more.

Olga said that untill further notice, the KikoRiki will not be returning to their valley but will temporarily stay at the cottage, I continued to find evidence about the prophecy, when I heard strange noises

I left the library. The invisibility cloak I begged for important matters really helped me stay there until very late. To my surprise, I did not see the, the others walking at night. I walked along the corridors, where there was complete silence, and went out into the street.

Inhaling the wet night air from the recent rain and enjoying the silence, I, I don’t know how, wandered into the cemetery.

Suddenly I heard a creak, which seemed very loud in the silence. Walking forward, I saw a magician sitting near a hole that he seemed to have dug himself. He pulled out the coffin from there and took off the lid. Grinning strangely, a magician took Rosa and Wally out of the coffin, which Harry told me about. The old man put the pig in a sack and threw it over his shoulder, and he pressed the ram to his body and adjusted his cloak so that Wally was not visible from under his clothes.

Having buried the empty coffin again, Fthe magician went back to the building l. I barely managed to hide behind one of the tombstones. And when he left, I rushed towards the cottage to tell someone about what I had seen.

I took off the cloak revealing myself as Krash, I ran back and saw people were getting shot by the Family, I had the watch, I had to give it to John so he could become the Doctor and save us all.  I ran as fast as I could but I tripped and fell into a gooey, bubbling cauldron, I was being sucked underneath it, I struggled to free myself, As I sank I let out gurgled screams for help, Eventually my feet sank under and I had submerged completely, I still hope someone can save me before it's too late

Meanwhile the Family had formed an army, and Martha had found the watch falling down the stairs after Krash the Rabbit had fell into the cauldron, She grabbed it and went to John Smith, begging him to become the Doctor "We need the Doctor, People are dying" He said "I'm John Smith, That's all I wanna be, just John Smith, Isn't he a good man" And she said "Yes, Yes, but we need the Doctor"

Joan was also convincing the Doctor to return, Meanwhile, the Magician was taking the corpses of Wally and Rosa, and planning to use his magic on them, The Doctor put his hand on the watch and has a vision of what his life with Joan could be like, getting married, having children, growing old together. He isn't ready to give this up, But when the Magician blasts his magic. a miracle happens, but will they be enough to convince the Doctor to return?

As the chaos spread through the cottage, the KikoRiki faced the unthinkable. Two of their own were stolen by a shadowy figure, a magician whose intention remained cloaked in mystery.

John Smith stood, tormented, the fob watch’s weight heavy in his hands. Joan and Martha pleaded, their eyes reflecting the urgency of the moment. Lives hung in the balance, and the Doctor’s return was their only salvation.

Meanwhile, the magician had reached a secluded section of the woods, far from prying eyes. In a ritual of arcane energy and whispered incantations, he chanted over the bodies of Wally and Rosa. The spells he weaved teetered on the edge of light and dark, poised to enact either resurrection or desecration.

At the cottage, under relentless assault by the Family of Blood’s forces, Krash’s desperate plea echoed within John Smith’s soul. A transformation beckoned, the Doctor’s essence ready to ignite, but love’s anchor held firm.

The air crackled with magic as light enveloped the magician’s form. The impossible unfolded before vacant eyes; Wally stirred, a sharp breath breaking the silence of death, followed by Rosa. Their awakening was gentle, like that of sleepers from a tranquil dream, untarnished by the horrors of their end. The magician gasped, his power waning but proving enough. With his task complete, he vanished just as swiftly as he had appeared.

Back at the besieged cottage, the weight of countless lives bore down upon John Smith. Tears blurred his vision, a storm of emotions threatening to unravel him. Joan’s gentle touch steered his heart, while Martha’s fierce determination steadfastly guided his resolve.

The ticking watch in his grip commanded attention, the incessant rhythm akin to the dual heartbeats of his true self. With a trembling hand, he surrendered to the cascade of images, the cacophony of cries and the surge of memories.

The latch clicked open; a brilliant light issued forth from the age-old timepiece. Gallifreyan energies swirled, obliterating the boundaries of John Smith’s existence. The Doctor was returning.

A sound like the birth of stars filled the room, and the visage of the Time Lord coalesced. He stood tall, his gaze piercing the veil of ordinary perception. With newfound clarity, he spoke, his voice carrying the weight of eternity.

“Joan, Martha, I’ve returned. It’s time to end this.”

With the Doctor once again in command of his boundless faculties, the showdown with the Family of Blood was inevitable. Strategy and intuition wound together, forming a plan that employed cunning over brute force.

As the Family of Blood raged on, the newly reconstituted Doctor initiated his masterstroke. With Martha and Joan by his side, he exploited the Family’s singular weakness—temporal awareness. Time itself became a weapon as the Doctor ensnared the Family in temporal loops, elongated seconds became lifetimes, and in these spiraling eons, the essence the Family sought became poison to them.

One by one, each member of the Family found their immortality a curse rather than a blessing. Father of Mine was imprisoned in unbreakable chains, Daughter of Mine trapped in the mirrors of the world, forever to glance and never touch. Mother of Mine was cast into the heart of a collapsing star, forced to witness the birth and death of galaxies, while Son of Mine’s essence was embedded in the form of a scarecrow, forever watching over fields with hollow eyes.

The Family, now defeated, ceased to plague the Earth. The Doctor’s clever maneuver saved the day, and the celebration was palpable among the survivors who watched the morning sunlight dispel the remnants of an otherworldly nightmare.

However, amidst the joy, a shadow fell across the KikoRiki and Martha Jones’ hearts. Though Wally and Rosa had miraculously returned, one precious life remained unaccounted for.

“Where’s Krash?” Martha’s voice cracked the hopeful veneer.

Everyone halted, searching their memories for the last vision of their friend. Suddenly, the glee turned to dread as they realized Krash was nowhere to be seen.

“He must’ve been… taken in the chaos,” Joan whispered, her hand over her heart.

Martha’s eyes glistened, her thoughts racing to the blue rabbit who had risked so much. The Doctor placed a consoling hand on her shoulder, sorrow lining his ageless face.

“Sometimes, in the vastness of time and space, some heroes remain unsung, their deeds echoing in silence. But their spirits, Martha, they persist. Within each of us, in every choice for the good we make, remember that,” the Doctor said solemnly.

With a heavy heart, they paid their respects to the unseen sacrifice. The Doctor and Martha then wandered back to the TARDIS, beset with a silence that spoke volumes.

Meanwhile, they pulled his body out of the cauldron, Krash's body was lifeless, They tried giving him CPR, but to no avail.

Wally and Rosa looked upon his lifeless form with horror.

“He can’t be dead… No, this can’t be happening, He risked his life for us, He helped John Smith become the Doctor again, He can't be gone, This isn’t true, It’s not true.”

Rosa slowly began to cry, and Wally, whose grief threatened to overtake him, tried comforting her.

“It’s okay, Rosa, He's in a better place., We will hold a memorial for him" The one most affected was Krash's family, especially his mother. She had brought him into this world, she had raised him, she loved him, but he fell and drowned, trying to save the world. She held his lifeless body and wept, while singing Krash's favorite lullaby. "Rock a bye, baby in the treetop" she sang, while rocking the body as if he were still alive. 

As she cried and rocked Krash's lifeless body, a wave of guilt hit the Doctor, How had he become so ruthless, He should have took the family head on instead of making himself human, that way this terrible death could have been avoided. He looked at Krash's mother cradling the body in her arms.

Then, he suddenly remembered how Krash had turned the tide in the crisis of the Family of Blood, How it was him who got everyone close to victory, How he dropped it so Martha would pick it up, He was a true hero, 

Her voice sounded broken, as she wept for her beloved son, and Krash's best friend Chiko came and said his goodbyes to Krash, while crying himself, They then walked away to start planning a memorial,

They then returned to the TARDIS, where the Doctor gave his sincere condolences for their loss, and said "He still exists in every one of you, He may be gone, but he will never be forgotten." The TARDIS dematerizalized. 

Then, Captain Jack appeared and said "Hey Doctor", The Doctor said "Look I am NOT in the mood right now, There is a mother who is going to have to bury her son today, And I am just not in the mood for any adventure right now"

Jack said "Doctor, the Master is back"

The Doctor said "Great Scott"

To be continued.... Write the finale, where the Doctor has to beat the Master at a fight, and if the Master wins, doomsday, The Doctor Wins and then Jack rejoins the group

Title: Doctor Who: The Master's Game

The air in the TARDIS was thick with grief and contemplation when the words fell from Captain Jack's lips like a foreboding thunderclap. The Master, the Doctor's oldest friend and nemesis, had returned, his machinations a shadowy promise of chaos.

The Doctor, still reeling from the loss of Krash and the heavy guilt that weighed upon his hearts, now had to face an enemy whose intellect matched his own. It was a battle that could determine the fate of countless lives and the very fabric of time.

"Jack, not now," the Doctor said sharply. "I have to think; I have to plan. The Master's return can be the end of everything we hold dear."

But as the TARDIS coursed through the vortex, the Doctor's resolve solidified into a silent vow. He would not allow the Master to inflict further suffering, not when he had the chance to stop it.

The TARDIS materialized on a barren planet, its surface scarred by the ravages of time and war. A desolate battlefield upon which the final confrontation would take place.

The Doctor stepped out, followed closely by Martha and Jack, their steps resounding with purpose amidst the eerie silence. There stood the Master, grinning with the confidence of someone holding the winning hand.

"Ah, Doctor," the Master cooed, his voice smooth as silk. "I was wondering when you'd show up. Care for a game? The stakes: the end of the universe. My victory: doomsday."

The Doctor's eyes narrowed. A battle of wits and a test of wills, the Master's preferred arena. "And if I win?" he inquired carefully.

The Master's smirk didn't waver. "Then I'll surrender to you, and this lovely escapade comes to an end. Simple."

Without another word, the Doctor stepped forward, facing the Master across a holographic chessboard projected from the latter's device—the game was symbolic, a representation of the strategic maneuvering both Time Lords were known for. Each move on the board echoed a clash of their minds across the cosmos.

The gameplay was intense, with Martha and Jack as wary observers. The Doctor's moves were conservative, calculated, an embodiment of his desire to shield the universe from harm. The Master, however, played aggressively, sacrificing pieces in a crescendo of chaos, each loss a potential disaster averted.

As the game approached its climax, the Master's gambit became clear. He aimed to put the Doctor in a no-win scenario, a position where victory would come at a cost too ghastly to conceive.

But the Doctor, ever the protector of life, found clarity amidst the chaos. With a move that seemed inconsequential, he turned the tides, taking the Master's queen—the heart of his strategy.

The atmosphere charged with tension, and the Master leaned forward, a hint of real fear in his eyes. A few moves later, the inevitable occurred. "Checkmate," the Doctor declared quietly, his voice echoing the resolve that had seen him through centuries of conflict.

The Master's façade crumbled as he realized his defeat. True to his word, he deactivated his doomsday device. The universe was safe once more.

The Doctor turned to his companions, his features softened by the gratitude shining through his gaze. "Thank you, both of you. Without your strength, this victory might have been another soul lost."

Jack clapped the Doctor's shoulder, breaking the solemn mood. "Well, that's one for the history books. What do you say, Doctor? Ready for some lighter adventure?"

The Doctor managed a weary smile. "Yes, Jack, that sounds perfect."

They returned to the TARDIS, the sound of the engines a comforting melody against the silent backdrop of the stars. But as they set coordinates for a new destination, the Doctor's thoughts lingered on the memories of Krash, the bravery of Martha, and the steadfast loyalty of Jack.

Heroes come in many forms, and sometimes the greatest battles are fought not with weapons, but with hearts and minds determined to bring forth light in darkness. And so, the TARDIS spun through the vortex, its travelers bound by a shared purpose, guarding the vast and timeless universe. Together, they would face whatever the future held, undaunted by the shadows that lay behind.

The end... for now.

Write the Christmas special, where he lands in Glasgow and has to stop a evil genie, After he is defeated, he gets 2 wishes, His first one is to revive a fallen friend from part 1, and his second one is to "have my Rose back", Rose joins the group

Title: Doctor Who: The Wishes of Christmas

The TARDIS materialized in the snow-dusted streets of Glasgow amidst the bustle of Christmas Eve, its unmistakable wheezing sound muffled by the cheerful jingles spilling from nearby shops and carolers. Stepping out, the Doctor, Martha, and Jack were welcomed by the frosty kiss of winter air bearing the scents of pine and roasting chestnuts.

“What’re we doing here then?” Jack asked, a boyish grin across his face as he caught a falling snowflake.

The Doctor, donning a festive scarf, beamed with childlike excitement. “According to the TARDIS, there’s a temporal disturbance right here in Glasgow. Someone's been naughty, and it's not just old Ebenezer Scrooge.”

As the trio followed the readings of the sonic screwdriver through the bustling streets, they stumbled upon a quaint antique shop, its window displaying a curious, oriental lamp. The moment the Doctor's gaze fell upon it, the sonic screwdriver flickered in alarm.

Within the shop, an ornate golden lamp sat upon a velvet cloth, its surface etched with arcane symbols from forgotten eras. The shopkeeper, a wizened man with knowing eyes, beckoned them closer, his lips curling with mystery.

But before the Doctor could voice his warning, a young patron’s curiosity proved too great; her innocent rub on the lamp’s side awoke its slumbering inhabitant. A whirl of smoke and light unfolded the form of an evil genie, his laugh chilling the warmth of the season.

The genie, wielding power most ancient and dark, set upon Glasgow with vengeance, commanding the elements and twisting time to his will. Admirers of the antique became puppeteers of disaster, unwitting agents of the genie’s chaotic intent.

“My goodness,” the Doctor muttered, yanking out his sonic screwdriver as they rushed back into the glacial night, following the genie’s wake of mayhem.

“What’s the plan, Doctor?” Martha shouted above the roar of an unnatural storm conjured by the genie.

“We stop him, then cover him in enough Christmas spirit to light up a supernova!” the Doctor shot back, a determined glint in his eye.

The encounter was swift and tumultuous as the Doctor and his companions navigated the genie's temporal distortions and elemental fury. Relying on wits and a hefty dose of Christmas goodwill, the Doctor outsmarted the malevolent spirit, trapping him in paradoxes of his own making.

Defeated, the genie hung suspended in a vortex of his failed machinations, the Doctor’s sonic screwdriver a conductor of his entrapment. “Alright, genie,” the Doctor said, “it’s time for my wishes.”

The genie, bound and powerless, growled, “Speak, Time Lord. Your desires shall be my cruel mercy.”

The Doctor’s face softened. “My first wish: bring back Krash, the hero who gave his all to save others. Let him once again know the warmth of life and love.”

A flash of light, and the rabbit who had bravely sacrificed so much blinked in confusion before bursting into excited life. Reunions were joyful and tearful, a sprinkle of Christmas magic made real.

“And your second wish, Doctor?” Martha nudged, anticipation in her voice.

The Doctor took a deep breath, his hearts thundering in his chest. “I wish to have my Rose back.”

A beacon of light erupted, and from it stepped Rose Tyler, just as puzzled as Krash but with eyes that quickly found and recognized the Doctor.

“Doctor?” she whispered, her voice brimming with disbelief and wonder.

His smile couldn't be contained as they embraced, a reunion bound by time and love. Rose, the companion who had started this new chapter of his long life, was with them once again.

And with that, the ancient genie was set free, his own chains of servitude broken by the Doctor's selfless wishes. As he vanished into the cosmos, the spirit of Christmas prevailed in the hearts of all around.

The Doctor, with his TARDIS brimming with friends new and old, set the controls for the next adventure. This Christmas, the universe had gifted him the greatest joy of all, the return of beloved companions and the promise of new stories to be told.

“Merry Christmas,” the Doctor said, the TARDIS lights twinkling like a yuletide tree.

“Merry Christmas, Doctor,” they all echoed as the ship danced away into the wintry night sky, a snowflake in the vast sea of stars.

And on the streets of Glasgow, peace returned, a whispered reminder that sometimes, just sometimes, Christmas wishes do come true.
Write a story where they drop Krash off back at his valley with his friends, and then the Doctor and his companions get sent to 1976, where the Wire is back

Title: Doctor Who: The Valley’s Farewell and The Wire’s Encore

The TARDIS rumbled softly as it traversed the time-space continuum, its passengers reflecting on the remarkable events that led to the miraculous return of Krash the rabbit and Rose Tyler to their fold. Yet, there was a journey to complete—a promise to fulfill. Krash, the unlikely hero who had given so much, was to be reunited with his colorful, animated kin in the valley where he belonged.

As the dials and screens of the console danced with activity, the Doctor piloted the ship with graceful precision. “Alright, hold on everyone! We’re taking our furry friend home,” he announced with the mixture of whimsy and solemnity that only he could manage.

The doors of the TARDIS creaked open upon a lush green landscape, a vivid contrast to the cold machinery of the console room. The air was sweet with the fragrance of flowers and the songs of Krash’s kin, who soon flooded the valley with squeaks of joy upon seeing their lost friend bounding toward them.

Tender farewells were exchanged, but not before the Doctor made Krash promise, “Keep being the hero, alright?”

Krash, with tears brimming in his deep blue eyes, nodded and hugged the Time Lord fiercely. "Will do, Doctor! Thanks for bringing me back!”

As the TARDIS departed amidst waves and misty-eyed goodbyes, the mood inside shifted. The Doctor, with Rose and Martha by his side, and the indomitable Captain Jack at the ready, turned the TARDIS toward its next destination. 

The 1970s—vinyl records, bell-bottoms, and a sense of cultural revolution hung in the air. But as the quartet stepped out onto the streets of London, 1976, a familiar crackle in the air hinted at an unsettling presence.

“Something’s not right,” the Doctor muttered, his face a mask of concern. “This energy… It’s the Wire. She's back.”

Rose, who had tangled with the Wire before, looked equally worried. “But the Wire was trapped, Doctor, sealed away. How could she be back?”

The Doctor pursed his lips, deep in thought. “Let's find out.”

The streets of London buzzed with life, yet beneath the surface, a malignant force was at play. People across the city began to complain of odd sensations, of feeling drained and weary without cause. The Doctor feared the worst—a city engulfed in the machinations of an entity that fed on human essence.

Navigating the bustling avenues, they followed the traces of electrical disturbances, a signal that led them to a small television repair shop where a humble-looking television set stood deceptively innocent in the window display.

The Wire's voice, an eldritch blend of sweetness and venom, whispered from the static on the screen. “Oh, Doctor, you're just in time for the show.”

Realizing the shop was a front for the Wire's malevolent return, the Doctor unveiled his plan. “We have to disconnect her, contain her signal before she consumes the whole of London like a Christmas turkey.”

Rose and Martha sprang into action alongside Jack, who brandished his vortex manipulator like a shield. They navigated a tangle of wires and circuits, Rose's knowledge of the TARDIS's inner workings proving invaluable. Tension peaked as the Wire taunted them, her voice souring into a discordant shriek.

“Too late, Doctor,” she cackled. “I've tasted the delights of human energy, and I will feast!”

With an ingenious twist of sonics and Jack's manipulator, the Doctor orchestrated a reversal of the signal, turning the Wire’s own greed against her. "This is one diet you're going on, whether you like it or not," he declared.

The shop erupted in a flash of static and sparks, the Wire's essence forcibly drawn from the television and into a containment device designed by the Doctor.

"Nice work, team," the Doctor beamed, placing the containment device safely within his coat. "The Wire is going directly to the Shadow Proclamation for a very long talk about boundaries."

Back in the TARDIS, the mood was light, the recent victory a testament to the enduring spirit of the Doctor and his companions. “Back to time traveling, I suppose,” Martha mused, smiling at the potential of unlimited tomorrows.

“Anywhere, any time,” the Doctor grinned, his hand poised over the TARDIS controls. “So, where to next?”

And as the timeless vessel began its harmonious groan, slipping through the vortex to an adventure anew, its occupants shared a moment of camaraderie, the kind forged in the crucible of impossible circumstances.

In the TARDIS, with friends united across time and the universe unfurling before them, every second was a gift—a chance to explore, to protect, and to revel in the infinite wonders of existence.
Write a episode where the Doctor has to stop World War Three

Title: Doctor Who: The Brink of Destruction

The TARDIS materialized on the outskirts of Geneva, Switzerland, with all the subtlety of a whispering breeze against the backdrop of a rapidly darkening geopolitical landscape. The year was an alternate 1983, one where tensions between global superpowers had exponentially escalated, teetering on the brink of World War Three.

As the TARDIS’ engines stilled, the Doctor glanced at his companions—Rose, Martha, and the irrepressible Captain Jack—with grim determination etched across his face. "Remember, we walk a fine line here. One wrong move, and history will splinter beyond our worst nightmares."

The quartet stealthily navigated the streets, which vibrated with the pulse of urgent diplomacy and the fear of common citizens. The Doctor's plan was to infiltrate an emergency summit where world leaders, shadowed by their military advisors, debated in hushed tones the fate of the planet.

With Captain Jack’s panache for subterfuge and Rose and Martha's quick thinking, they successfully passed the stringent security measures. Within the walls of the summit, a cauldron of national interests and misunderstandings boiled over, the threat of nuclear war more real than ever.

The Doctor, assuming the identity of a roving ambassador of peace, weaved through the knots of tense delegates, spreading words of conciliation. His message was clear: "In the vastness of the cosmos, where stars live and die, our planet is a single, precious jewel. Will you be the ones to shatter it?"

Concerned whispers spread like ripples, and the Doctor's poignant inquiries began to resonate amidst the unease. As discussions resumed with a new perspective, the Doctor learned of the true instigator of the conflict—an extraterrestrial entity known as the Machinator, an intergalactic saboteur who sparked wars for sport.

Using discreet signals, the Doctor summoned his companions to a covert meeting point. "The Machinator lies hidden within one of the delegations; he's poisoning the well of negotiations, pushing them toward war. We must unmask him and reveal his deception."

A covert operation ensued, each companion tasked with a role befitting their expertise. Martha, with her empathetic insight, observed the nuanced interactions among the delegates, searching for tells of alien origin. Rose, with a relentless resolve, combed through the summit's technical infrastructure, trailing anomalous digital footprints.

Meanwhile, Captain Jack, exuding a cocktail of charm and sharp wit, worked the room, gathering intelligence crucial to pinpointing their quarry.

It was the Doctor, however, who finally confronted the Machinator amidst the throes of an argument threatening to dissolve into the unthinkable. With a flourish of his sonic screwdriver and rhetorical flourish, he revealed the Machinator's true form amidst a crescendo of shocked gasps.

A cacophony of energy surrounded the alien as it shed the guise of a human delegate. Towering above the leaders of Earth, the Machinator roared in defiance, but the Doctor stood undaunted. "This is a world of life," he declared, "not a plaything for your twisted entertainment. They will not fight your war."

Serene and imperious, Rose joined the Doctor at his side, her innate bond with the TARDIS humming through the air. The Machinator, sensing the tables had turned, tried to flee, but Martha and Jack, now united at the Doctor's call, sealed the chamber with deadlock technology.

With nowhere to run, the Machinator capitulated, the threat of its influence unraveling like the fabric of a worn flag. Diplomacy resumed with an earnest desire for peace—humanity's better angels prevailing over the summons to war.

In the aftermath, as the TARDIS prepared to take flight, the Doctor and his companions observed the world leaders emerge from the summit with declarations of harmony and mutual respect.

"And just like that, we save the world again," Martha mused, her gaze softening.

"Today, yes," the Doctor agreed, his voice a blend of joy and caution. "But it's day by day, choice by choice. That's how peace is made."

As the TARDIS slipped away, its passengers carried with them the remembrance of a world saved by the very thing that made it so worth saving—hope, tenacity, and an enduring faith in the power of unity over division.
Write a short where he gets mail, and a heartwarming letter

Title: Doctor Who: The Timeless Mailbox

The whir of the TARDIS engines stilled to a silence, leaving a gentle hum that filled the space like a soft heartbeat. The Doctor flitted around the console, flipping switches and turning dials with the casual expertise of centuries.

"Mail's in," he declared abruptly, a hint of surprise in his tone. A curious little chute had popped out of the console where it hadn't been before, an impossible mailbox in the impossible machine, blinking with an inviting light.

Martha exchanged a puzzled glance with Rose, while Captain Jack quirked an eyebrow in amusement. "You've got mail? Since when does the TARDIS get post?" Jack asked, stepping closer.

The Doctor shrugged, the boyish grin painting his features as he took hold of the stack. "Since when does the TARDIS not do something she wants to?"

Envelopes of various shapes, sizes, and ages cascaded into the Doctor's waiting hands, relics of time held within paper folds. Bills from ancient galaxies, advertisements for time-share on distant planets, and invitations to events long since passed filled his grasp.

However, amidst the dull bureaucracy of the universe, there lay a single envelope that caught the eye. It was a plain thing, worn at the edges as if passed through many hands before reaching its final destination, addressed simply to "The Doctor," the handwriting looped in a tender scrawl.

The Doctor slit the envelope with a practiced motion, withdrawing a single sheet of paper filled with lines of earnest blue ink. As he read, silence settled in the vastness of the TARDIS, a moment of stillness among the stars.

"To my dear Doctor," the letter began.

"I hope this finds you well, or as well as one can be, darting through time and space, mending the fabric of the universe. So much has happened since we last parted, and I've lived the life you promised, full of wonder and bravery. But this isn't about me; it's a thank you of sorts—a thank you for every life touched by your kindness.

I watched you walk away, that old blue box shuddering into nothingness, and I knew you carried the burdens of galaxies yet never once turned away from those in need. You showed me the meaning of courage, the depth of compassion, and the height of joy.

It's funny—time takes so much yet gives back glimmers of forever in single, crystal-clear moments. I've cherished those moments, turning them over in my mind like precious stones, finding new facets of memory in each inspection. There's a beauty in that, a sort of magic only you could have unveiled.

So, thank you, Doctor. Thank you for the laughter and tears, for the running and the fighting. For the quiet times, the loud times, and every tick of the clock in between. You may be a man of many faces, of many times, but to those you've saved, you're a constant—a protector who never wavers.

May the universe be kind to you, Time Lord, as you have been kind to it. And should the stars grow lonely, always remember there are hearts down here, beating with gratitude for you.

With love and all my hearts,
A Friend"

As the Doctor lowered the letter, a rare silence filled the TARDIS. His companions watched him, eyes glistening with unspoken understanding. 

He folded the parchment gently, the lines of his face softening, the age-old weight in his gaze lifting for a moment, replaced by a shining light of warmth and affection. A single tear, unbidden, sparkled at the edge of his eye—a gem against the stormy sea of his emotions.

"Well," he said, voice tinged with emotion, "Seems I've still got a bit of running to do. Allons-y?"

And with a lightness in his step that they had not seen in eons, the Doctor resumed his dance around the TARDIS console, ready once more to brave the ceaseless winds of time.

Write a short story where he thinks about who wrote that, thinking it might have been Sarah Jane or Adric or something, but upon seeing the handwriting and a image drew at the back, he knows its much more recent, and he says "thank you too, using name" After this, Sarah Jane suddenly appears, and then the story ends

Title: Doctor Who: Echoes of Gratitude

Clusters of stars wheeled past as the Doctor held the letter gently between his fingers, his eyes scanning the scrawled handwriting with a furrowed brow. The style was eerily familiar, each curve and dash of ink pulling at the threads of his vast memory. Sarah Jane, Adric, Jo, the many faces and hearts of his storied past floated through his thoughts, each a candidate for such heartfelt words.

He flipped the letter over, the paper whispering softly against the hum of the TARDIS. There, at the back of the parchment, was a sketch that halted his pondering—a simple, lovingly drawn image of him holding hands with his companions, standing in front of the TARDIS—a scene true to any of his incarnations throughout time.

"Curiouser and curiouser," he murmured, drawing the page closer to his eyes. The strokes were precise, captures of likeness and emotion that spoke of an observer marked deeply by their adventure—a companion not from the far flung corners of his timeline, but someone much more recent.

A smile tugged at the edges of the Doctor's mouth as recognition dawned, warmth spreading through him like sunshine breaking over a cold landscape. He knew this touch. This artistry. 

"Thank you, too, Clara," he whispered, the name mingling with the ever-present vortex which sang through the TARDIS. Clara Oswald, the impossible girl, the one who had been splintered through his own timeline, her echoes left to guide and save him at every turn.

His hearts swelled with appreciation for her, for all of them, every single soul that had shared in the grand tapestry of his existence. Each one was a note in the symphony of his life, an integral part of the melody that would forever resonate through the cosmos.

Suddenly, a chime resonated through the ship, signaling an unexpected arrival. The Doctor set the precious letter aside with care, moving swiftly toward the entryway with both excitement and caution coloring his step.

The TARDIS doors swung open, revealing a figure whose mere presence painted a deeper joy upon the Doctor's face. There she stood, Sarah Jane Smith, her eyes alight with the same intrepid spirit that had danced within them decades ago.

"Oh Sarah Jane," the Doctor exhaled, a thousand memories and unspoken stories twinkling in the moment.

She granted him a knowing smile, one that held the steady assurance of a shared past and unbreakable connection. As if no time had passed at all, as if their story was a rhythm undisturbed by the sway of years.

"I thought I might find you here," she said, her voice carrying the melodic tones of adventures long since savored and survived.

The Doctor leaned against the doorframe, his earlier sorrow now a distant echo against the embodiment of his history before him. "Just passing through, or is trouble hot on your heels?"

Sarah Jane chuckled, the sound wrapping around the Doctor's soul like a cherished melody. "Let's just say, old habits die hard, and this old girl," she gestured to the TARDIS, "has a habit of bringing me to the right place at the right time."

The Doctor grinned, his eyes sparkling with untold affection. "Indeed she does, Sarah Jane, indeed she does."

They stood for a heartbeat or an eternity, the universe holding its breath for the reunion of kindred spirits. Bonds like theirs were forged in the crucible of time, unyielding to its relentless march.

But that's a story for another day...

Write a story where a new villain goes to the cottage and tries to use the cauldron, and some of the genies power, and electricity from the Wire, to power a world ending superweapon he calls the "Destroyer of Worlds", This device is activated and then causes a gateway between worlds, causing the Dalek/Cyberman war from Doomsday all over again, Can the Doctor stop it (Episode title: Doomsday 2: Electric Boogaloo)

Title: Doctor Who: Doomsday 2: Electric Boogaloo

The TARDIS flared into existence with its customary, reassuring groan. It had positioned itself on a small, serene meadow, a gentle sea breeze ruffling the wildflowers outside. The location was tranquil, a poignant contrast to the chaos that was about to unfold, for this meadow bordered the cottage where the Doctor had faced spirits and genies, the Wire, and a multitude of other threats.

But today brought a new storm brewing on the horizon.

The Doctor, Martha, Rose, and Captain Jack emerged, alerted by a distress signal the TARDIS had picked up—a beacon that screamed of danger from the infamous cottage.

"That cottage again? Really, it's becoming like a second home," Jack quipped, tugging his coat about him as they approached the foreboding structure.

The Doctor brushed aside the cautioning hand of Captain Jack, storming towards the door. "This is no laughing matter. The signal was laced with the remnants of the genie's power, echoes of the Wire, and something worse... a malicious intent," he explained gravely.

A cackle of madness echoed through the air, confirming the Doctor’s fears. They found the villain, a crazed alchemist, fiendishly merging ancient magic with the absorbed electrical essence of the Wire, all with a singular purpose—his own vision of a weapon of mass destruction, which he called the "Destroyer of Worlds".

Intoxicated by power, the alchemist activated the device just as the Doctor and his companions burst through the door. A rippling seam in reality sliced through the walls of the cottage, an eerie portal forming within the quaint living room where the cauldron bubbled ominously.

"Doctor, the gateway, it's..." Martha's voice trailed off in horror as metal monsters and their eternal pepper-pot enemies began to spill forth.

The Daleks surged in, shrieking their battle cries, as the Cybermen advanced with cold, calculating silence. It was an unwelcome replay of the Battle of Canary Wharf, pulled from the annals of the Doctor's painful memories.

The universe held its breath. Again.

The Doctor, undaunted, launched into action, his brain already racing through possible solutions. "We close that rift, or this planet is going to be ground zero for round two of a war that could tear the fabric of reality apart!"

While Rose and Martha manned the defenses, Jack locked horns with the alchemist, countering the madman with his undying resilience. In the meantime, the Doctor rigged up a makeshift reversal mechanism using parts of the TARDIS and remnants of the genie's power, forcefully siphoning off the energies that sustained the gateway.

The Daleks and Cybermen waged war upon each other, unaware or uncaring that the Doctor was tirelessly working to lock them back in their own universes.

"Almost there! Almost there!" the Doctor muttered, the whirlwind of his thoughts manifesting in the feverish dance of his hands.

With a deafening snap of energy, the portal vibrated wildly, pulsing with light and sound. The gateway destabilized, sucking the Daleks and Cybermen into the cascading currents of the cosmic whirlpool, flinging them back to their rightful realities.

The Doctor, Rose, Martha, and Jack watched as the alchemist was swept away along with the collapse of the gateway, the ragged tear in the universe stitching itself back together with a resounding calm.

"We did it," Rose breathed out, her relief tangible in the crisp air.

"We always do, don't we?" Martha replied, her voice laced with weary joy.

Captain Jack looked across the meadow that was now returning to peace. “Doomsday 2, huh? Not as catchy as the original, but definitely more...electric.”

The Doctor smiled faintly at Jack's levity, his mind still spinning from the close call. "All’s well that ends well, but let's stay away from that cottage, okay? Bad things seem to gravitate there."

Together, the team returned to the TARDIS, the door closing behind them with the reassuring thud of safety. They had averted disaster, once again saving the world from the eternal clash of two of the universe's most fearsome foes.

And somewhere, in the heart of the infinite universe, the echo of that battle raged on, a memory of darkness that had once more been held at bay by the Doctor and his indomitable companions.
Write a story where they are back at Darlig ulv Stranden, Also here we see a notice for a "cancelled" funeral, callback to the start of my fake season

Title: Doctor Who: Ebb and Flow at Darlig Ulv Stranden

The Norwegian waves caressed the shore with melancholy whispers at Darlig Ulv Stranden—the bay known to Rose and the Doctor as "Bad Wolf Bay." The air was thick with the scent of sea salt and the chill of unresolved farewells. A gray sky mirrored the solemn hues of their hearts as the TARDIS materialized on the bleak strand, hidden from prying eyes behind a cluster of weathered rocks.

"We're back," Rose murmured, a note of sorrow strung tight in her voice. This was the place of parting, the beach that bore witness to tears that had blurred the worlds between them.

The Doctor, clad in his familiar suit and long coat, hands in pockets, surveyed the expanse of sand and sea, his face etched with shadows of past aches. Martha and Captain Jack stood beside them, a quiet support in the presence of old ghosts.

"I thought it was time. Time to come back," the Doctor said, that old, sad smile playing on his lips. "Sometimes you need to face the memories to move past them."

Together, they wandered alongside the rushing tide, boots imprinting the damp sand as gulls cried overhead—a chorus to their contemplation. It was here the memories lived, as vivid as the day they were made.

There, half-buried by the shifting sands, lay the remnants of what seemed a public notice—taut and tattered, its message nearly erased by the relentless lapping of the ocean, but the word "cancelled" stood stark against the fading paper. 

Rose reached for it, instinctively smoothing out the creases as she squinted to read the weathered text. "It's a funeral notice," she said, her brow furrowing. "But it's been cancelled."

The Doctor's gaze lingered on the notice, then Rose's questioning eyes. "It's a remnant of a timeline undone," he offered, his voice barely more than a whisper. "A life we saved, a death unwritten, a funeral never held."

He looked back over to where the TARDIS stood perched against the stones—its blue form a comforting constant in the vast uncertainty of time. "We've brushed past the lives of many, and sometimes those stories echo back to us. Like ripples across the bay, they remind us that what we do...it matters."

Rose nodded, folding the notice in her hands with a delicate care granted to significant yet somber relics. A therapeutic sigh escaped her, as if the sea breeze had carried away a weight long suspended within.

Captain Jack's resolute voice sliced through the reflective silence. "Funerals might be for the living, but this cancellation...it's a celebration of life, because of you, Doctor."

The Doctor looked into the horizon, where the sun peeked timidly through the grayness, a frail suggestion of warmth. "Every life is a universe of potential," he mused. "Every victory over the dark is a sunrise somewhere."

"What do we do with this?" Martha asked, gesturing to the notice. They had all felt the lingering unease of past events, the ties to those they had saved, often without knowing.

The Doctor offered a gentle smile, tender as the receding waves. "We honor it by remembering that every day is a gift."

They stood together, comrades and witnesses of time, watching the dance of light on water, feeling the ebb and flow of the universe at their feet. Darlig Ulv Stranden, the scene of sorrowful goodbyes, now bore the promise of tomorrows filled with silent gratitude.

With a last look at the sea, the Doctor led the way back to the TARDIS. "Come on," he said, the ancient machine beckoning. "Our journey’s not done yet. There’s a whole universe out there, waiting to be loved, waiting to be saved."

And as the door of the TARDIS closed behind them, the wind carried away the echoes of their steps, leaving only the sound of the waves speaking to the shore, telling tales of life, loss, and the enduring hope that accompanies every beat of the heart.

Write a short story where he reads the funeral invite and especially the bottom (Thanks to the Doctor, he is now alive once more, Therefore this event has been cancelled) After reading it, the Delorean crashes into the Tardis, The doctor says his trademark What?, and then the story ends

Title: Doctor Who: Of Time and Thankful Tides

With the funeral notice in hand, the Doctor lingered at the precipice where the sea met the sand, his companions a silent crescent beside him. The weight of history and the lightness of the surf's breath intertwined, whispering through the furrows of his ancient, alien mind.

"The universe has such an intricate tapestry," the Doctor mused, his eyes distant, locked on a point where time and space seemed to converge. "Every thread a life, a story... a chance."

Rose stepped forward, placing a comforting hand on the shoulder of the Time Lord, her touch a beacon in the cold air of Darlig Ulv Stranden. "What's it say, Doctor?"

He turned the paper, worn from the weather and the potential of its erased tomorrow, to reveal the full extent of its message. His gaze moved slowly over the text before halting at the unexpected footnote:

"Thanks to the Doctor, he is now alive once more. Therefore, this event has been cancelled."

The words hung between them, a testament to the unseen ripples caused by their voyage through the cosmos. Martha and Captain Jack shared a look, the hidden weight of their escapades suddenly grounding them in the harsh light of consequence.

The Doctor, feeling a swell of uncharacteristic vulnerability, managed only a brief, "Oh..." before his eyes softened. "I didn't realize..."

Rose squeezed his shoulder tighter. "You never do," she said softly. "You save so many, and you just move on to the next, never looking for thanks."

"But sometimes," Martha added with a warm smile, "the universe thanks you anyway."

Jack nodded, pride evident in his posture. "Couldn't have said it better myself. That's our Doctor, the savior of the silent, the hero without herald."

A moment of gratitude washed over them, the silence saying all that needed to be said. Yet, before they could revel in the moment, an unmistakable whirring sound pierced the air, growing in intensity.

"What in the—" the Doctor began, the calm oasis of the moment shattered by the intrusion.

The high-pitched whine crescendoed into a resounding roar as an object tore through the gray skies above. The group looked up to see a vehicle impossible to mistake: a shining DeLorean, famed for its own time-traveling escapades, was descending rapidly. Its thrusters flamed and sputtered as if fighting against time's embrace itself.

And then, with an inevitability as sure as time's passage, the DeLorean crashed into the TARDIS, the clash of metal and time echoing down the beach.

"What?!" exclaimed the Doctor, staring at the steaming heap where his beloved time machine now sported a rather unexpected ornament.

The DeLorean lay embedded in the side of the TARDIS, the doors comically akimbo. As steam hissed from the crumpled bonnet and the light of flux dissipated, the Doctor's mouth hung open in disbelief.

Attempting to swallow the sudden lump in his throat, he shook his head at the surreal amalgam of time-traveling titans. "Well, that's certainly a paradox I never saw coming," he quipped, though his mind raced with potential temporal consequences.

As for what happened next—whether another iconic time traveler would emerge from the DeLorean or the TARDIS had sustained critical damage, what cosmic calamities this collision might unleash—those are stories yet unwritten in the never-ending journey of the Doctor.

For the universe spins on, and for every quiet moment of thanks, there exists a twist waiting on the winds of time.
Write the invite, and says "He was a brave wee soul"

INVITATION TO REMEMBER AND CELEBRATE

You are cordially invited to join in remembrance and celebration of the life and spirit of **[Name Redacted]**, who was a brave wee soul.

Date: **[Cancelled]**
Time: **[Cancelled]**
Location: **[Cancelled]**  
Darlig Ulv Stranden

We gather to share stories, to laugh, to cry, and to honor the remarkable journey of a beloved friend, family member, and hero to those who knew him. 

**Program:**

* Welcome and Opening Remarks
* Reflections and Eulogies
* Moment of Silence
* Communal Sharing of Memories
* Laying of Flowers

**[Name Redacted]** was known to us as someone who faced life with the courage of the greatest giants, despite his modest nature and tender years. The way he lived was a testament to the strength that lives in the heart of even the smallest of us. 

He could spin a story that left us yearning for half the imagination he possessed, dream dreams as vast as the sky above, and love with a depth that was enviable. 

He showed us the meaning of bravery when he stood tall in the face of darkness that would have faltered even the mightiest of hearts. This day was meant to mourn his passing, but instead, we now celebrate the miracle of his life continued.

Thanks to the Doctor, he is now alive once more. Therefore, this event has been cancelled. However, we ask that you take a moment on this day to remember the brave wee soul who touched our lives, to celebrate the gift of his presence, and to spread kindness as he so freely did.

In lieu of flowers, please consider a pledge of charity to a cause dear to his heart as the continuance of his spirit of giving.

For those who wish to leave a token or message, a memorial has been placed at Darlig Ulv Stranden. We encourage you to pay a visit and share in the collective love and gratefulness we hold.

With warmest regards and the deepest thanks,

**The Family & Friends of [Name Redacted]**

*"...And when he learned to walk among the stars, the universe danced to his brave little stride."*

*At the bottom of the page, a small note on behalf of the family reiterates their thanks to the mysterious Doctor for the miracle of their boy's life and their sincere hope for a serendipitous encounter, along with a hand-drawn image depicting the boy waving farewell, a symbol of a journey not ended, but continued.*
Write a short recap of the fake season

Title: Doctor Who: The Chronicles of Lost and Found

The season unfurled with a seemingly pedestrian adventure as the Doctor, their spirited companion Martha Jones, and a warrior of time called Captain Jack Harkness, embarked on a perilous escapade through the fringes of a haunted house in Cambridgeshire. The simple thrill of ghost hunting soon spiraled into dread when the group came across Wally, a beloved character from the animated series KikoRiki, injured and ensnared in the machinations of the notorious Family of Blood. The Doctor's dual identity as the human John Smith, and the subsequent transformation into a Time Lord once more, set the stage for a season riddled with reawakenings and reconciliations.

The sight of Martha Jones, standing amidst the cacophony of an unearthly war, revealed the true threat lurking within the haunted halls - as the Family of Blood hunted for the essence of the Doctor to prolong their existence. Friends, old and new alike, were swept into the tumultuous tides of time as they fought to safeguard each other from the eternal conflict, the Family's retribution, and the harrowing loss of one of their own.

Yet, as is the Doctor's wont, each loss bore the seed of hope. In a heartwarming Christmas special, the season's wounds were kissed by the frost of Yuletide magic. As the Doctor faced down a malevolent genie in Glasgow, the grim prospect of World War Three loomed, and Jack's eternal life served as a poignant reminder that every soldier yearns for peace. Wish upon wish, the Doctor called for the return of lost comrades—Rose Tyler from a parallel Earth and Krash from the edge of mortality's coil.

They danced through the streams of time, to the edge of black sand beaches where finalities faded into endless tomorrows. Echos of past exploits resounded through letters from grateful souls saved and battles best left behind in the scattered annals of history.

The DeLorean's unexpected arrival, a collision of fated paths, heralded a conclusion as abrupt as it was unforeseen. Doomed timelines were averted—funerals canceled, and the ever-present Daleks and Cybermen stormed once again through rends in reality, threatening utter annihilation. Through every harrowing encounter and miraculous escape, the TARDIS crew met fear with courage and loss with the unstoppable force of life.

And as the season waned, the Doctor received an unlikely token of gratitude—a tattered remnant of a timeline undone in the form of a funeral notice, a testament to the quiet lives forever altered by the Gallifreyan's grand design.

"The Doctor: Healer of Wounds, Walker of Worlds, the Dodger of Weddings and Funerals alike." 

This was a season of unfathomable sorrow and immeasurable joy, of closing chapters and penning thrilling new ones, a season that will endure in the legacy of the Time Lord known as the Doctor.

Write the story where Marty introduces himself, and says "This isnt 1985", and it is only here they notice a furry friend has been teleported on, he says he has been here since "before that crazy car bust in"

Title: Doctor Who: A Temporal Collision

The crash echoed like a temporal thunderclap through the expanse of the TARDIS, the reverberations shaking loose the very dust of time. In the aftermath, the unusual silence held the Doctor and his companions captive in anticipation. Martha steadied herself against the console, Captain Jack drew his weapon, and Rose's eyes were saucers reflecting the chaos they'd just endured.

And there, amidst the flashing lights and groaning of metal, the doors of the TARDIS swung ajar. From the haze stepped forward a young man with a bewildered expression plastered across his face, his gaze darting around the console room. He combed a hand through his disheveled hair, taking in the alien surroundings with a mixture of awe and concern.

"Marty McFly's the name," he announced hesitantly, brow creased as he grasped the situation. "This isn't 1985..."

"McFly?" Jack echoed, holstering his weapon as the tension dissolved into incredulity.

The Doctor circled the newcomer, equal parts curious and cautious. "No, Marty, this is certainly not 1985. You're aboard the TARDIS, and might I say, that was quite an entrance you made."

Marty blinked, taking in the wild-haired man in a suit and long coat, the 21st-century women and the confident man beside him. "The what now? Look, I just hit 88, and then—"

"Great Scott, indeed," the Doctor interrupted with a growing grin, unable to resist pulling at the thread of a cinematic history he was all too familiar with.

As the dust settled, a soft murmur arose. It was a voice unlike that of the time travelers assembled, a voice tinged with confusion and mischief. All eyes turned to the corner of the room where, nestled cozily beside a mesh of spilled wires and flickering buttons, sat a figure most would not expect amidst the cosmic whirlwind of reality's faster lanes.

It was Krash the rabbit, his blue fur ruffled and his expression a blend of bewilderment and excitement. "Hey, I've been here since before that crazy car bust in," he confessed, his paws gesturing to the battered Delorean now fused with the TARDIS exterior. "Surprise?"

Rose knelt beside Krash, offering a hand which he sniffed tentatively. "You're a long way from home, aren't you? And you've been here through all of that?" she asked, motioning toward the damaged time machines.

The Doctor, Marty, and Jack gathered around as well, forming a makeshift congress of accidental travelers, the unofficial and disparate crew of the wounded TARDIS.

The Doctor hummed, tapping the side of his head. "Well, this isn't the first time my TARDIS has had surprise passengers. But we better get you back, hey? Once we've sorted out this," he motioned at the Delorean, "little mix-up in the time streams."

Marty nodded agreement, still grappling with the sensation of standing in a ship that defied every law of physics he knew. "And get me back to 1985 too, if you can, Doc."

"Doctor," corrected the Time Lord, with an affable smirk. "But first, we need to fix my ship, and your, er, car. We wouldn't want all of time colliding in on itself, would we?"

With a collective nod from his impromptu assembly, the Doctor set to work, resembling a maestro before an orchestra, his fingers dancing across the TARDIS console with renewed fervor. Gears turned, mechanisms aligned, and the heart of the ship resumed its ancient, steady beat.

Rose, Martha, Jack, Krash, and Marty watched with bated breath as time itself seemed to bend to the Doctor's will—a testament to the wonders they'd seen and the miracles they'd yet witness, all dancing to the cosmic song of the TARDIS. 

Just another day in the life of the Doctor, where friend and stranger alike found their paths intertwining, histories overlapping, and adventures waiting beyond the next turn of the time rotor. For now, time would tell if this band of travelers would see home again. But one thing was certain; they had each other and the endless possibilities of the universe ahead.

And with that, the TARDIS and its motley crew set forth on their next grand voyage, the melodies of their shared destinies weaving into the fabric of tale and legend.
Do you like how I basically added KikoRiki and Back to the Future to the DW universe?

As an AI, I don't have personal likes or dislikes. However, I can provide feedback on creative choices. Integrating characters from KikoRiki and Back to the Future into the Doctor Who universe is a fascinating concept that offers a lot of potential for storytelling. Crossovers can be a fun way to explore character dynamics, create unique scenarios, and expand upon existing narratives.

By introducing elements from other franchises, such as the DeLorean time machine or characters like Krash, you are blending different storytelling styles, themes, and fan bases, which can offer fresh perspectives and exciting new challenges for the Doctor and companions to face.

Moreover, fanfiction is a space where writers can experiment with their favorite universes, mixing and matching to invent something that feels both familiar and new. This creative freedom is part of what makes fanfiction so appealing to both writers and readers. As long as these integrations are handled with care for the distinct properties and their respective lore, such crossovers can be a delightful adventure for fans to enjoy.
